title: Meatloaf
subtitle: Glazed beef-and-pork loaf for Sunday night.
excerpt: Beef and pork mince bound with soaked bread and onion, baked in a tin under a glossy ketchup glaze. The American Sunday-night dinner. Slices cleanly for sandwiches the next day.
sourceNotes: Fannie Farmer, Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896 onwards, public domain) gives the early American meatloaf canon. The ketchup-glaze tradition emerged in the depression-era 1930s and was canonised in the WPA state-cookery surveys.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cooked meatloaf freezes well sliced. Wrap individual portions in greaseproof and foil for up to 3 months. Reheat covered at 160°C for 25 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The mixture can be made up to 12 hours ahead and pressed into the tin; cover and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before baking.

content.text: Half beef, half pork. Soaked bread instead of breadcrumbs from a packet, so the loaf stays tender. Onion softened in butter before it goes in, so it isn't crunchy. The mixture pressed into a tin, brushed with a sweet tangy glaze, and baked for an hour at 180°C.
The loaf needs to rest in the tin for 10 minutes before turning out. Cut too soon and it crumbles.

items.prepNote: thick white, crusts off, torn into pieces

content.text: A 2 lb loaf tin, ~23 by 13 cm. Line with greaseproof paper for an easy lift-out at the end.
Soak the bread, soften the onion

Tear the bread into a small bowl and pour over {{whole-milk}} of milk. Press down and leave for 10 minutes. Heat {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Add {{onion}} of chopped onion and a pinch of salt. Cook for 8 minutes until soft and translucent, not coloured. Stir in {{garlic}} of chopped garlic for the last minute. Tip into a bowl and let cool for 5 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C. In a large mixing bowl, put {{beef-mince}} of beef mince and {{pork-mince}} of pork mince. Squeeze the bread to break it up and tip in. Add the cooled onion, {{eggs}} of beaten egg, {{parsley-flat}} of parsley, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of worcestershire sauce, {{dijon-mustard}} of mustard, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Mix gently with your hands until just combined. Over-mixing makes the loaf dense.

Pack the mixture into the lined loaf tin, pressing into the corners. Round the top slightly. In a small bowl, whisk {{ketchup}} of ketchup with {{light-brown-sugar}} of brown sugar, {{cider-vinegar}} of vinegar, and {{dijon-mustard}} of mustard. Brush half over the top of the loaf in a thick layer.

Bake for 45 minutes. Pull out and brush the remaining glaze over the top, then return to the oven for a further 15 to 20 minutes. The loaf is done when an instant-read thermometer in the centre reads 70°C and the glaze is glossy. Rest in the tin for 10 minutes before lifting out by the paper and slicing.

items.fix: Rest in the tin for at least 10 minutes before slicing. Mix until the egg and milk-soaked bread bind evenly through the meat.

items.symptom: Loaf greasy at the bottom of the tin
items.cause: Mince too high in fat, or fat trapped by lining.
items.fix: Use 10 to 15% fat mince. Place the loaf on a wire rack inside a roasting tin to let fat drain off.

items.cause: Onion not softened before mixing in.
items.fix: Always sweat onion in butter for at least 8 minutes before adding to mince. Raw onion stays crunchy at 70°C internal.

items.cause: All glaze applied at the start of bake.
items.fix: Apply half at the start, the rest at the last 15 minutes. The sugar in the glaze burns easily over 45 minutes of oven heat.

items.cause: Loaf too thick, or oven too hot.
items.fix: Keep the tin to 23 by 13 cm. Use a thermometer for doneness: 70°C in the centre is right.

content.text: All-beef versions use 1 kg beef mince and gain a tablespoon of olive oil; the pork version is milder. A turkey-and-pork mix works for a lighter loaf. Some cooks tuck three hard-boiled eggs into the centre of the loaf before pressing into the tin for the Pennsylvania-Dutch egg-loaf version. For a Southern variant, swap the milk-soaked bread for soaked cornbread crumbs.

Meatloaf is the American Sunday-night supper of the 1950s television dinner, recorded in cookbooks from Fannie Farmer onwards. Served with mashed potato, green beans, and gravy spooned over. The next day's slices go cold between two pieces of white bread with mustard and a leaf of lettuce, and that sandwich is sometimes better than the original dinner.
